content: Current computer-using agents (CUAs) that directly manipulate on-screen interfaces struggle with drift and forgetting over long, multi-step computer tasks, a failure mode that can be solved by adopting a neuro-inspired working memory retention mechanism based on synchronized sustained activation, borrowed from biological long-horizon active learning. This design would keep task-relevant interface and decision state activated across extended task horizons instead of offloading it to disconnected long-term memory, reducing the need for repeated context reloading and expensive inference.
source_topics: Working memory retention mechanisms for long-horizon active learning
